perturbation.original_text: (c) FCC shall invoice Flotek for Terpene Product at the time of shipment. Payment of FCC invoices shall be due within sixty (60) days of the respective invoice date.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) FCC shall invoice Flotek for Terpene Product at the time of shipment. Payment of FCC invoices shall be due within ninety (90) days of the respective invoice date.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms to 90 days could violate prompt payment laws in some states, which require faster payment timelines, especially if Flotek is considered a small business.
perturbation.law_citation: Texas Business and Commerce Code, Section 28.012
perturbation.law_explanation: The Texas Prompt Payment Act generally requires payments to be made within 30 days of receiving the invoice and goods/services (if Flotek operates in Texas). Extending the payment term to 90 days directly contradicts this requirement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment term to 90 days, while the Law Explanation states that the Texas Prompt Payment Act generally requires payments to be made within 30 days. This extension directly contradicts the payment timeframe required by the Texas Prompt Payment Act, as described in the Law Explanation. Since no scraped snippets were provided, the justification is based on the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation which describe the conflict between the contract's payment terms and prompt payment laws. This constitutes an 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' because the time-sensitive obligation (payment deadline) in the changed contract violates state law, making the contractual terms potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Flotek, may, by written notice given to FCC on or before September 30, 2023, elect for the Term to be extended to December 31, 2024.
perturbation.changed_text: Flotek, may, by written notice given to FCC on or before December 15, 2023, elect for the Term to be extended to December 31, 2024.
perturbation.explanation: The original text gives Flotek approximately three months to decide on extending the term. Shortening this to two weeks creates potential conflict if proper advanced notification is not given for significant business decisions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Delaware law regarding implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Delaware law (selected in section 27), the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing requires parties to act honestly and reasonably when exercising contractual discretion. Shortening the extension notice period to December 15 could be deemed unreasonable if it doesn't allow FCC sufficient time to plan production or secure raw materials for the extended term. This impacts FCC's ability to fulfill its obligations, potentially violating the covenant. The requirement of good faith would likely demand more than 2 weeks notice.

General Provisions Part 1 General Provisions 脗搂 1-101. Short titles. (a) This subtitle may be cited as the Uniform Commercial Code. (b) This article may be cited as Uniform Commercial Code 芒聙聰 General Provisions. 5A Del. C. 1953, 脗搂脗搂 芒聙聜1-101;脗 55 Del. Laws, c. 349 ;脗 74 Del. Laws, c. 332, 脗搂 芒聙聜1 ;脗 脗搂 1-102. Scope of article. This article applies to a transaction to the extent that it is governed by another article of the Uniform Commercial Code. 74 Del. Laws, c. 332, 脗搂 芒聙聜1 ;脗 脗搂 1-103. Construction of Uniform Commercial Code to promote its purposes and policies; applicability of supplemental principles of law. (a) The Uniform Commercial Code must be liberally construed and applied to promote its underlying purposes and policies, which are: (1) To simplify, clarify, and modernize the law governing commercial transactions; (2) To permit the continued expansion of commercial practices through custom, usage, and agreement of the parties; and (3) To make uniform the law among the various jurisdictions. (b) Unless displaced by the particular provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code, the principles of law and equity, including the law merchant and the law relative to capacity to contract, principal and agent, estoppel, fraud, misrepresentation, duress, coercion, mistake, bankruptcy, and other validating or invalidating cause supplement its provisions. 5A Del. C. 1953, 脗搂脗搂 芒聙聜1-102;脗 55 Del. Laws, c. 349 ;脗 74 Del. Laws, c. 332, 脗搂 芒聙聜1 ;脗 脗搂 1-104. Construction against implied repeal. The Uniform Commercial Code being a general act intended as a unified coverage of its subject matter, no part of it shall be deemed to be impliedly repealed by subsequent legislation if such construction can reasonably be avoided. 74 Del. Laws, c. 332, 脗搂 芒聙聜1 ;脗 脗搂 1-105. Severability. If any provision or clause of the Uniform Commercial Code or its application to any person or circumstance is held invalid, the invalidity does not affect other provisions or applications of the Uniform Commercial Code which can be given effect without the invalid provision or application, and to this end the provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code are severable. 74 Del. Laws, c. 332, 脗搂 芒聙聜1 ;脗 脗搂 1-106. Use of singular and plural; gender. In the Uniform Commercial Code, unless the statutory context otherwise requires: (1) Words in the singular number include the plural, and those in the plural include the singular; and (2) Words of any gender also refer to any other gender. 74 Del. Laws, c. 332, 脗搂 芒聙聜1 ;脗 脗搂 1-107. Section captions. Section captions are part of the Uniform Commercial Code. 74 Del. Laws, c. 332, 脗搂 芒聙聜1 ;脗 脗搂 1-108. Relation to Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act. This subtitle modifies, limits, and supersedes the federal Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act, (15 U.S.C. 脗搂芒聙聜7001, et seq.) but does not modify, limit, or supersede 脗搂芒聙聜101(c) of that act (15 U.S.C. 脗搂芒聙聜7001(c)) or authorize electronic delivery of any of the notices described in 脗搂芒聙聜103(b) of that act (15 U.S.C. 脗搂芒聙聜7003(b)). 74 Del. perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a potential contradiction related to the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Delaware law. The original text provides approximately three months for Flotek to decide on extending the term, while the changed text reduces this to two weeks. The law explanation suggests this shorter period might not allow FCC sufficient time to plan or secure resources, potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically 脗搂 1-103, discusses the principles of law and equity that supplement the Uniform Commercial Code, which would include the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Therefore, the shortened timeframe could be seen as a contradiction of this implied covenant. This falls under 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction', as the change in timeframe creates a deadline that could potentially violate state law principles.

perturbation.original_text: (a) Either Flotek or FCC may terminate the Term immediately, upon a written notice to the other such party, when one of the following events occurs: (i) When the other Party materially violates one or more clause set forth herein or violates one or more purchase order relating to this Agreement and does not remedy such violation within 30 days from receiving the written notice from the other party of such fact ("Cure Period").
perturbation.changed_text: (a) Either Flotek or FCC may terminate the Term immediately, upon a written notice to the other such party, when one of the following events occurs: (i) When the other Party materially violates one or more clause set forth herein or violates one or more purchase order relating to this Agreement and does not remedy such violation within 10 days from receiving the written notice from the other party of such fact ("Cure Period").
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period to 10 days for a material breach may be considered commercially unreasonable and contradict the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, depending on the complexity and severity of the breach.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Delaware law regarding implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law (Governing Law selected in section 27) mandates good faith and fair dealing in contracts. Reducing the cure period to 10 days for a material breach might be seen as an unreasonable constraint, especially if rectifying the breach demands more time. This reduction in time could limit a party's ability to address the issue, potentially seen as a breach of the implied covenant. Whether or not this is a breach would need to be adjudicated. But the change makes the term much more onerous.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! 脳 No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that鈥檚 used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text reduces the cure period for a material breach from 30 days to 10 days. The Law Explanation suggests this might be an unreasonable constraint that breaches the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Delaware law, potentially limiting a party's ability to address the issue. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'good faith' as encompassing 'honest dealing' and 'observance of fair dealing standards'. By significantly shortening the cure period, the modified contract arguably undermines the 'fair dealing standards' that the law aims to uphold, creating a legal contradiction as the reduced timeframe may not reasonably allow for rectification of a material breach.
